title.zh: 读完Anthropic的"圣经"，我把餐厅进销存从2小时砍到10分钟
title.en: After Reading Anthropic's "Bible," I Cut Restaurant Inventory from 2 Hours to 10 Minutes

excerpt.zh: 既然试图用系统去规范"人性"的To-Do List行不通，那就去管最实在的东西：钱和货。
excerpt.en: If software can't regulate human nature, manage something more honest instead: money and inventory.

zh.text: 进销存的烂账
既然试图用系统去规范"人性"的To-Do List行不通，那就去管最实在的东西：钱和货。
中小餐饮门店的进销存，是一笔堪称魔幻的烂账。像海底捞这种级别的连锁，可以用极度规范的Barcode（条形码）去追踪每一批货。但普通门店面对的是极度非标的供应商。一颗生菜，今天是按棵记，明天可能就按斤记。损耗、结余全是一笔糊涂账。
月末查账的时候，老板通常只能看到一个极其粗颗粒度的营收和毛利。至于具体的成本明细？不知道。哪怕这个月某个门店莫名其妙少了200斤肉，也只能淹没在厚厚的纸质入库单里，无迹可寻。
两小时录不完一页单
为了拿到最真实的数据，我写了第一版进销存系统。逻辑很简单，我把所有的SKU内置好，做成下拉菜单，每天进什么货，让后厨对着入库单往里填就行。
当时的厨师长叫张鹏辉，我们都叫他辉哥。辉哥这个人，有点意思——退伍军人出身，做过铁路电工，后来才进了餐饮。那种当过兵的人特有的做事风格，严谨、不抱怨、能吃苦，在他身上体现得非常明显。辉哥拿到我的系统后，搬了一把廉价的白色塑料藤椅，坐在收货台旁边，腿上摆着一叠花花绿绿的收货单——粉色的、黄色的、白色的，手写的和热敏打印的混在一起，叠了厚厚一沓。他就那么认认真真地，开始录入。
结果，整整一个下午，他花了两个小时，连一页单子都没录完。
中国餐饮的SKU实在太庞杂了。我们做火锅还算好的，门店有500多个SKU，有些正餐门店甚至能逼近1000个。每天要进60到80种货，辉哥需要拿着手机，在长长的列表里一个一个选规格、填重量、称重、拍照、上传。
更隐蔽的是命名不一致的问题。供应商的收货单上写的是"肥牛"，但我们系统里的SKU叫"雪花牛肉"——两个词指的是同一种东西，辉哥却在列表里怎么找都找不到。他要么凭经验脑补，要么随手填一个，要么就卡在那里不动。我意识到，这不只是"录入太慢"的问题，而是一个更深的数据标准化困境：供应商的命名体系和我们的SKU体系之间，需要一套完整的别名映射，才能让数据真正准确起来。

zh.alt: 进销存系统品类选择界面
zh.caption: 品类浏览界面——辉哥要先在这里找对大类，才能进入对应的SKU列表录入。

zh.text: 看着辉哥濒临崩溃的表情，我意识到我又犯了书呆子的错：这种反人类的交互设计，在第一线根本活不下去。更让我纠结的是，我心里清楚这些数据对老板有多重要，但采集数据的代价是让一个已经在厨房工作了十几个小时的人，下班后还要对着屏幕录入。为了数据准确性，让辉哥多付出这些，值得吗？这个问题在我脑子里一直没有消失。
向"简单技术"妥协
既然纯手工录入不行，那加点AI总行了吧？作为AI狂热爱好者，我脑子里立刻冒出了一个极其赛博朋克的方案：AI识图 + AI语音纠偏。
我想象中的画面很酷：拍个照，系统自动识别单据；如果有错别字，辉哥不需要拿手去敲键盘，按住麦克风说一句"嘿，这个可乐的乐识别错了，不是勒索的勒，是快乐的乐"，系统就能自动改过来。我还特意接了讯飞的API，觉得这套丝滑的语音交互简直完美。
系统做出来了，然后立刻在现实中摔得粉碎。
我忽略了一个极其致命的物理条件：后厨和收货区，是一个极其嘈杂的环境。风机声、锅碗瓢盆的碰撞声、供应商的扯皮声交织在一起，语音识别在真实噪音面前简直是个笑话。语音纠偏不仅没能帮上忙，反而频频报错，逼得辉哥最后还是得满手油污地去点手机键盘。

parts.text: 这让我感到非常挫败。我一直习惯去解构和抽象事物的本质，但这次在泥坑里打滚，让我真正具象化了那篇被圈内奉为圭臬的文章——Anthropic发布的

zh.text: 这篇文章的核心哲学极其克制，甚至可以说是反直觉的：

items: 你能用LLM Workflow解决的，不要拿Agent Loop解决；
你能拿硬编码解决的，不要拿LLM Loop解决；
你能不造软件解决的，你就不要造一个SaaS。

zh.text: 我之前就是典型的"拿着锤子找钉子"，为了显得系统有"AI含量"，强行堆砌花里胡哨的交互。其实，面对满手油污的厨师长，最简单、最直接的技术，才是最优雅的。
我立刻砍掉了所有自嗨的语音功能，向"简单技术"妥协。但我的克制不止于此——我甚至砍掉了后端。

这个决定的背后，是我和Gemini彻夜讨论的结果。那天我们用第一性原理追问了一整晚：前端到底是什么？后端到底是什么？它们各自存在的本质意义是什么？最后我们得出一个让我豁然开朗的结论：后端的意义只有一个——把需要在不同设备之间共享的状态，安全地存起来，再取出来。如果前端自身就能完成所有的计算，数据只需要跨设备持久化，后端就变成了一层多余的传话筒。直接用数据库加上数据库的Edge Function，整个架构就可以从三层压缩成两层。
但这个场景里，数据必须被存下来，辉哥需要一个交互界面，所以最终的架构极其扁平：只有前端，加上数据库。那唯一的一点点计算量——调用AI图片识别（OCR）的那几行逻辑——我把它扔进了Supabase的Edge Function里。几毫秒跑完，不需要为了这一点计算量专门起一个后端服务器。整个系统架构极其轻薄，没有服务器需要维护，没有中间层需要对齐，出了问题直接在数据库和前端之间找。
我放弃了复杂的智能体推理，转而用最基础的AI图片识别（OCR），加上极其传统的硬编码逻辑，把纠偏分成了三档：

items: 绝对匹配：识别结果和系统SKU完全对得上，直接绿灯放行，系统静默处理。
疑似匹配：识别出了关键字段，但存在形近字（比如"可乐"识别成了"可钉"）。系统用硬编码逻辑把它关联到"可乐"上，但给出一个高亮提醒，让辉哥人工扫一眼确认。
完全失控：错得离谱，或者根本无法与现有SKU匹配（比如"可乐"识别成了"雪碧"）。系统直接放弃治疗，提示"这活儿我干不了"，强行要求手动录入。

zh.alt: AI识图+填入物品清单的录入界面
zh.caption: 拍一张收货单，AI自动填入物品清单，辉哥只需确认高亮项。

zh.text: 数据改变了什么
就是这套去魅后的、极其克制的"组合拳"，彻底改变了局面。
辉哥每天的工作流变成了：拿起收货单拍个照，对着货物拍个照，系统瞬间拉出列表。他只需要花几秒钟，扫一眼系统高亮提示的几个"疑似错误"的词，点两下确认，结束。

zh.caption: 每一笔采购记录清晰可查，供应商、金额、时间一目了然。

zh.text: 原本每天需要耗费2个小时、甚至逼得后厨压根不想录入的进销存工作，被压缩到了10分钟。
直到今天，这个系统依然在门店里运转。每个月末，老板不仅能看到清晰的毛利，还能精准定位到每一笔细分成本，甚至能反向去优化供应商的价格。
第一份月报出来的那天，老板盯着屏幕沉默了很久。那个月，有一家门店的账上莫名其妙"消失"了200多斤肉。这不是小数——那批肉以前就是淹没在纸质单据堆里、永远查不清楚的那种损耗，现在系统把这笔异常直接挑了出来。
更有意思的是老板随后用系统做的一次分析。他把所有SKU按"销量×毛利率"做了四象限分类：高销量高毛利的是核心产品，重点保护；低销量低毛利的是累赘，直接削减；高销量低毛利的品类，是最值得压供应商价格的地方。就凭这一张四象限图，他用了不到一个月，把整体采购成本压缩了接近2%。对于一家年营收过千万的门店，这2%是货真价实的真金白银。

zh.caption: 门店总览与价格趋势看板——老板第一次能实时看到9家门店的采购异常。

zh.text: 后来我去研究了美团、天财商龙这些行业巨头，他们的进销存系统庞大且周密，但根本没有向下兼容到这种极度DIY、替一线员工抠出这1小时50分钟的微观体验。
让我最意外的，不是老板有多开心，而是后来接手这套系统的人——老板娘。她是学金融风控出身的，对技术一窍不通，但她有两个特质：极其细心，极其较真。她开始拿着AI工具来维护和迭代这个系统——最开始是改改报表格式，后来是加新的品类，再后来开始自己提功能需求、自己找AI写逻辑。如今这个系统已经跑了将近一年，数据越来越准，她已经在谋划下一步：做预测性分析，提前判断哪些食材下个月的采购量应该调整。
创业这件事，悄悄地从"我要做一个产品"变成了"我帮别人做一个工具，他们拿着AI把它继续造下去"。我从一个创业者，变成了一个布道者——不是在传播技术，而是在传播一种认知：AI不是替代你干活的机器，而是一个让没有技术背景的人也能独立建造的工具。
很多人觉得AI创业就是要用最先进的模型去颠覆行业。但在真实的泥土里，我认为克制才是最高级的炫技。你能用两行If-Else解决的问题，就别去唤醒大模型。我们的角色应该是problem solver，而不是problem creator，也不是technology user。我们只做一件事：用最简单的方式，解决最真实的问题。这是我的哲学。

en.text: The Inventory Mess
If software can't regulate human nature, manage something more honest instead: money and inventory.

Inventory management at small and mid-sized restaurants is a spectacularly messy problem. A chain like Haidilao can use standardized barcodes to track every shipment. But ordinary restaurants deal with wildly non-standardized suppliers. A head of lettuce might be counted by the head today, by the kilogram tomorrow. Spoilage and surplus are permanent mysteries.

At month-end, owners typically see only a coarse-grained revenue and gross margin figure. Specific cost breakdowns? Unknown. Even if a location mysteriously ran short 200 jin of meat that month, it's buried under a pile of paper receiving slips with no way to trace it.
Two Hours, One Page

To get real data, I built version one of an inventory system. The logic was simple: pre-load all SKUs into dropdown menus, have kitchen staff fill in incoming shipments against the paper receiving slips.

The head chef at the time was Zhang Penghui — everyone called him Hui Ge. He's an interesting character: a military veteran turned railway electrician turned restaurant cook. That particular discipline ex-soldiers carry — meticulous, uncomplaining, durable — showed clearly in how he worked. Hui Ge pulled up a cheap white plastic wicker chair beside the receiving dock, laid a thick stack of colorful receiving slips across his lap — pink ones, yellow ones, white ones, handwritten and thermal-printed mixed together — and started entering data with the same steady focus he probably brought to every job before this.
Two hours later, he hadn't finished a single page.

Chinese restaurant SKU counts are staggering. Our hotpot restaurant was on the simpler end — 500+ SKUs. Some full-service restaurants push toward 1,000. With 60 to 80 incoming product types per day, Hui Ge had to scroll through an endless list on his phone, selecting specifications, entering weights, photographing, uploading — one item at a time.

There was a subtler problem underneath that: naming inconsistency. The supplier's receiving slip said "féiniú" — a generic term for thinly-sliced beef. Our system's SKU said "xuěhuā niúròu" — wagyu-style marbled beef. Same product, completely different names. Hui Ge would search for the supplier's term, find nothing, and either guess, fill in something wrong, or just freeze up. I realized this wasn't only a "slow entry" problem — it was a data standardization problem at a deeper level. Every SKU needed a full alias map linking supplier naming conventions to our own before the data could be trusted.

en.caption: Category browsing screen — Hui Ge had to navigate to the right category first before reaching the SKU entry list.

en.text: Watching Hui Ge's expression approach breakdown, I realized I'd made another textbook mistake: an interface this hostile to the user can't survive on the front line. But there was a more uncomfortable feeling underneath — I knew this data was genuinely valuable for the owner, but collecting it meant asking someone who'd already worked twelve hours in a kitchen to sit down and enter records after his shift. Was that trade worth making? I couldn't answer it cleanly, and the question stayed with me.
Surrendering to Simplicity
If pure manual entry didn't work, what about adding AI? As an AI enthusiast, I immediately imagined something gloriously cyberpunk: AI image recognition + AI voice error correction.

The vision was cool: take a photo, system auto-reads the receiving slip; if there's a misread, Hui Ge just holds the mic and says "Hey, that Cola was misread — wrong character" and the system fixes it. I even integrated iFlytek's API. It felt perfect.
The system shipped. Then reality smashed it.

I had ignored a brutally obvious physical constraint: the kitchen and receiving area are deafeningly loud. Exhaust fans, clattering cookware, suppliers arguing over deliveries — voice recognition in real kitchen noise is a joke. The voice correction didn't help at all; it just generated errors, forcing Hui Ge to poke at a phone screen with oil-covered fingers anyway.

parts.text: That failure forced me to actually internalize something I'd read but hadn't felt — Anthropic's essay
, which the AI community treats almost like scripture.

en.text: The core philosophy is remarkably restrained — almost counterintuitive:

items: If you can solve it with an LLM Workflow, don't use an Agent Loop.
If you can solve it with hardcoded logic, don't use an LLM Loop.
If you can solve it without software, don't build a SaaS.

en.text: I'd been the classic hammer-looking-for-nails builder, stacking flashy AI interactions to make the system feel sophisticated. But faced with a chef's oil-covered hands, the simplest, most direct technology is the most elegant.
I cut all the self-indulgent voice features immediately. But I didn't stop at simplifying the AI — I cut the backend too.

That decision came out of an all-night first-principles conversation with Gemini. We kept pushing on the same question: what is a backend, really? What problem does it actually solve? Working backward from first principles, we landed on this: a backend's only irreducible job is to safely store and retrieve state that needs to be shared across devices. If the frontend can handle all the computation, and the database can expose its own API through an Edge Function, then the backend is just a redundant relay — a layer that exists to look professional, not because it solves a real problem. You can collapse three layers into two.

But in this case, data had to be stored, and Hui Ge needed an interface. So the final architecture was radically flat: frontend plus database, nothing else. That left one small piece of computation — the OCR call to the AI image recognition API. I dropped it into a Supabase Edge Function. A few milliseconds to run, no dedicated server required. The whole system was featherlight: no servers to maintain, no middleware to align, no layers to debug across. When something broke, you looked at the frontend or the database. That's it.
I replaced the complex agent reasoning with basic OCR plus hardcoded correction logic, split into three tiers:

items: Exact match: recognized text maps cleanly to a known SKU. Green light, processed silently.

Probable match: key fields recognized, but with a visually similar character substitution (e.g., "Cola" misread as a near-homophone). Hardcoded logic links it to the right SKU, but highlights it for Hui Ge to glance at and confirm.
Full failure: completely wrong, or can't be matched to any SKU (e.g., "Cola" read as "Sprite"). System gives up gracefully: "I can't handle this one" — requires manual entry.

en.alt: AI image recognition entry interface with auto-filled item list
en.caption: Photograph the receiving slip, AI fills in the item list — Hui Ge only confirms the highlighted entries.

en.text: What the Data Changed
This de-glamorized, maximally restrained combination changed everything.

Hui Ge's daily workflow became: photograph the receiving slip, photograph the goods, system pulls up the list. Spend a few seconds scanning the highlighted "probable" items, tap twice to confirm. Done.

en.caption: Every purchase record clearly traceable — supplier, amount, and timestamp at a glance.

en.text: The inventory work that used to take 2 hours — or drive kitchen staff to skip it entirely — was compressed to 10 minutes.

The system is still running in the restaurant today. Every month-end, the owner sees a clear gross margin breakdown with precise cost-line visibility — granular enough to renegotiate supplier pricing.

When the first month-end report came in, the owner went quiet for a long time. One location had somehow "lost" over 200 jin of meat that month. That's not a small number. This kind of loss had always been invisible before — buried in paper receiving slips, permanently unaccountable. The system surfaced it directly.

What he did next was more interesting. He ran a four-quadrant analysis on every SKU — plotting sales volume against gross margin. High-volume, high-margin items were core: protect them. Low-volume, low-margin items were deadweight: cut them. High-volume, low-margin items were the most valuable targets: negotiate harder with those suppliers. Armed with that single quadrant chart, he compressed total procurement costs by nearly 2% within a month. For a restaurant doing millions in annual revenue, 2% is real money.

en.caption: Store overview and price trend dashboards — the owner could see procurement anomalies across all 9 locations in real time for the first time.

en.text: I later studied how Meituan and Tiancai Shanglong handle this at scale — their systems are comprehensive and sophisticated, but none of them have optimized downward to this level of micro-UX: the 1 hour and 50 minutes you can give back to a line cook.

What surprised me most wasn't how happy the owner was. It was who took over the system next: his wife. She has a background in financial risk management — zero technical knowledge. But she has two qualities: meticulous attention to detail, and a stubborn streak. She started using AI tools to maintain and iterate on the system herself. First, small things — tweaking report formats. Then adding new product categories. Eventually, drafting feature requests and working with AI to write the logic. The system has now been running for nearly a year. The data keeps getting more accurate. Her next goal is predictive analytics — anticipating which ingredients need more or less stock in the coming month before the orders go out.

Quietly, "starting a company" had become "building a tool for someone else, and watching them keep building it with AI." I had shifted from founder to evangelist — not for the technology, but for a mindset: AI isn't a machine that does your work for you. It's a tool that lets someone with no technical background build something real.

A lot of people believe AI entrepreneurship means deploying the most advanced models to disrupt industries. But in the actual dirt, I believe restraint is the highest form of craft. If you can solve it with two lines of if-else, don't summon a large language model. Our job is to be problem solvers — not problem creators, and not technology users. We do exactly one thing: solve the most real problems using the simplest possible means. That's my philosophy.
